drills.scenario: Maya runs a Shopify pet brand selling a no-pull dog harness. She's filming a 4-minute Facebook video ad for a cold audience and wants to structure it using the agency's core formula.
drills.question: Order the structural steps of the Three-Step Video Ad Formula as they should appear in the video.
options: Stop the scroll in the first 3-10 seconds with a hook
Use at least one EDIE element (Educate, Demonstrate, Inform, or Entertain)
Place a soft CTA within the first 60-90 seconds
Repeat the CTA again before the end, telling viewers exactly what to do next
drills.explanation: Three-Step Video Ad Formula: (1) Stop the Scroll in the first 3-10 sec, (2) deliver value via EDIE, (3) Call to Action. For videos ~3 min+, a soft CTA should appear within the first 60-90 seconds and CTAs should repeat across the video since only a portion watch to the end.

drills.scenario: A SaaS founder built a tool that turns any blog post into a polished PDF ebook in one click. He wants a short Facebook video ad and is deciding which EDIE element to lead with.
drills.question: Which EDIE element best fits this product?
options: Educate — run a 9-minute mini-webinar teaching content repurposing
Demonstrate — a 30-second screen capture showing the tool turn a post into a PDF
Entertain — a parody skit unrelated to the product
Inform — a slideshow of statistics about content marketing
drills.explanation: Demonstrate shows the product in action and works great for technology/software brands ('we love to see gadgets, and we love to see them work'). The module cites a 30-second demo of software turning a blog post into a PDF ebook as the canonical Demonstrate case.

drills.scenario: A real estate coach sells a paid program that funds students' first house flips. He's designing a cold-traffic video ad and asks how to come up with the hook.
drills.question: How should he derive the hook, per 'working backward from the offer'?
options: Pick whatever hook gets the most views, regardless of the offer
Start from the end offer, identify its 'missing ingredient,' then build the hook from that (e.g., show a real flip, then reveal funding is the missing piece)
Open with a hard 'Buy my program now' CTA so cold viewers know the price immediately
Copy a competitor's hook and attach his own offer at the end
drills.explanation: Working backward from the offer: define the end goal, identify the offer's 'missing ingredient' the video pre-frames, then derive the hook from the offer. Showing a real before/after flip builds credibility and pre-frames the paid offer (the funding) as the logical next step.

drills.scenario: A local gym owner posts a Facebook video ad to a cold audience. The video is 45 seconds: a logo animation, upbeat music, and a voiceover that repeats 'Join FitZone today — sign up now!' five times. It gets impressions but zero sign-ups.
drills.question: What is the core problem, and the fix?
options: The video is too short — extend it to 18 minutes to build trust
It's a straight 'buy now' CTA to cold traffic with no value — educate/demonstrate and deliver upfront value first, then pivot to the CTA
There are too many CTAs — remove all but the final one
The music is the issue — remove all audio since video matters more than audio
drills.explanation: Pitfall: don't run a video that's just a straight 'buy now' CTA to cold traffic — it won't work. For cold/unaware audiences you must build authority and rapport by educating and delivering upfront value first, then pivot to sell. (Audio is actually MORE important than video, so removing sound is wrong.)

drills.scenario: A skincare brand is about to publish a 5-minute cold-traffic video ad. The marketer runs through the pre-publish checklist before launching.
drills.question: Which of these are required best-practices the module's checklist calls for? (Select all that apply.)
options: The first 3-10 seconds stop the scroll
Audio is strong/memorable, assuming sound-on autoplay
A portal (visual or auditory) is used to reset attention before the decision
The video must be exactly 5 minutes — no shorter, no longer
A soft CTA appears within the first 60-90 seconds, with CTAs repeated
drills.explanation: The pre-publish checklist requires a scroll-stopping open, strong audio (Facebook autoplays sound-on), a portal to reset attention, and a soft CTA in the first 60-90 sec with repeated CTAs. Option 3 is wrong: there is NO arbitrary length rule — best performers range 20 sec to 18 min; match length to brand/product.

drills.scenario: A plumbing company films a video ad. The owner wants viewers to feel moved from 'just scrolling' into a receptive, decision-ready state. His videographer suggests opening outside the shop: 'Hi, I'm Fred... come inside, I want you to meet some people,' with the camera following him through the door as background music starts.
drills.question: What technique is this, and why does it matter?
options: It's a portal — moving the viewer from one place/state to another resets attention and primes them to make a decision
It's a testimonial — third-party proof that builds trust
It's the EDIE 'Entertain' element — its only purpose is to make people laugh
It's a hard CTA — it tells viewers exactly what to buy
drills.explanation: This is a portal (7 Power Questions, Q7): moving the prospect visually/audibly from one place/state to another resets attention so they're more open to change. The plumber walk-through with music starting is the module's canonical auditory/visual portal example. Without a portal, the audience is less likely to make a change.

drills.scenario: A bootstrapped coffee brand has a tiny budget and a strong creative idea but can't afford a film crew. The founder also already has several decent videos on the brand's YouTube channel. He asks where to spend his limited resources.
drills.question: What does the module advise?
options: Spend the entire budget on high production value — polish is what converts
Invest in the idea/creativity over production value, and mine existing assets like the YouTube channel before producing anything new
Never reuse old footage — every ad must be freshly shot to look professional
Make every testimonial as polished and cinematic as possible to maximize trust
drills.explanation: Invest in the idea, not production value (a ~$700 Dollar Beard Club video drove millions). Mine existing assets first — the agency CEO watches every video on a new client's YouTube channel for reusable footage. Over-polished testimonials actually read as LESS trustworthy than something shot on an iPhone.

drills.scenario: A course creator wants to launch a new offer to a COLD audience and, over time, build audiences of engaged viewers she can retarget down the funnel. She has a compelling 5-minute video.
drills.question: Which campaign objective does the module recommend for this goal?
options: Conversions objective only — optimize straight for purchases from cold traffic
Video Views objective — get the video to most-likely-to-watch people and build retargetable engaged-viewer audiences
Reach objective — maximize impressions regardless of who watches
Engagement on a boosted post with no audience-building plan
drills.explanation: The module recommends the Video Views objective to get your video in front of people most likely to actually watch and to build audiences of viewers you can retarget along the customer journey (unaware to customer). It's powerful for launching an offer to a cold audience.
